title: How Mitchell Hashimoto uses AI in development | R. Hunter Harris posted on the topic | LinkedIn
author: R Hunter Harris
text:
Mitchell Hashimoto shipped a production feature with AI and shares every prompt, failure, and manual fix. This is what real AI-assisted development looks like. Most AI coding content shows the highlight reel. Mitchell Hashimoto does the opposite here: https://lnkd.in/eAJB8kjc . He documented his complete process building a macOS auto-update feature for Ghostty, including the sessions where AI failed completely. Key patterns from his approach: Plan before you prompt. Hashimoto researched Sparkle's documentation and macOS titlebar APIs before his first AI session. He knew the backend approach and had directional UI ideas. This background knowledge allowed the creation of clear prompts which allow AI to excel. Break work into reviewable chunks. He started with UI-only work, moved to backend, then integration. Each session had a specific scope. This made it possible to review, understand, and iterate. Use AI for inspiration, not blind acceptance. When the agent produced UI mockups, Hashimoto evaluated them, kept directional elements, and manually polished. He often prompts AI, discards everything, and rewrites manually, using it as a creative catalyst. Know when to walk away. After four failed attempts to fix a critical titlebar bug (chats 11-14 in his thread), Hashimoto pivoted. He switched to an overlay approach instead. AI isn't always the answer. Clean up between sessions. He dedicated multiple sessions just to documentation, refactoring, and code organization. Better structure helps future AI sessions perform better. He calls these "anti-slop sessions." Test rigorously. He had AI generate multiple simulation scenarios: happy path, errors, edge cases. The simulation code was "gross" by his admission, but functional for testing. Perfect is the enemy of good. All told? This feature was complete in 8 hours for $15.98 in token costs. But speed wasn't the main benefit. Instead, it was the ability to delegate work while doing other things, allowing true multitasking.
Perhaps also relevant would be his post about how he approaches "building large technical projects": https://mitchellh.com/writing/building-large-technical-projects
